title: Derivatives and Rates of Change
objectives: Define the derivative of a function at a point using the limit definition
Compute derivatives using both forms of the difference quotient
Interpret the derivative as instantaneous velocity and rate of change
Determine the equations of tangent and normal lines to curves

devotional.title: New Things Springing Up
devotional.content: "See, I am doing a new thing! Now it springs up; do you not perceive it?" The derivative captures the essence of change at a single instant—the precise moment when something new springs into being. Just as God works in ways that are ever new, the derivative reveals the instantaneous rate at which quantities transform. In studying calculus, we develop eyes to perceive the continuous unfolding of change that God has woven into the fabric of creation.

content.text: In Section 1.3 we computed slopes of secant lines and watched them converge as the two points drew closer together. In Section 2.1 we gave that convergence a name: the *limit*. Now we take the final step. Armed with the full machinery of limits—limit laws, algebraic techniques, and even the rigorous $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition—we return to the tangent problem and the velocity problem and solve them *completely*.

What emerges is the single most important concept in all of calculus: the **derivative**. It unifies the tangent problem and the velocity problem into one idea, and it opens the door to an extraordinary range of applications—from physics to economics, from biology to engineering. Every computation in this section will use the limit definition directly. This is deliberate. Before we earn shortcuts, we must understand what the shortcuts are shortcutting.
The Tangent Problem, Revisited

Recall the central question from Section 1.3: given a curve $y = f(x)$, what is the slope of the tangent line at a particular point? We answered this question informally by computing slopes of secant lines through $(a, f(a))$ and a nearby point $(a+h, f(a+h))$ and letting $h$ shrink toward zero. The slope of each secant line was
$$m_{\text{sec}} = \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h},$$
and we observed that these slopes seemed to converge to a single number as $h \to 0$. At the time, we lacked the formal tools to make this precise. Now we have them.

content.title: Slope of the Tangent Line
content.content: The **tangent line** to the curve $y = f(x)$ at the point $P = (a, f(a))$ is the line through $P$ with slope $$m = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h},$$ provided this limit exists.

content.text: The expression $\dfrac{f(a+h)-f(a)}{h}$ is called the **difference quotient**. It measures the average rate of change of $f$ over the interval from $a$ to $a+h$. The limit transforms this average into an *instantaneous* rate—the slope of the curve at the single point $x = a$.

content.title: Tangent line to a parabola
content.problem: Find the equation of the tangent line to $f(x) = x^{2}$ at the point $(1, 1)$.
content.solution:
We compute the slope of the tangent line using the limit definition:

$$\begin{aligned} m &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(1+h) - f(1)}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{(1+h)^{2} - 1}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1 + 2h + h^{2} - 1}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{2h + h^{2}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{h(2 + h)}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} (2 + h) \\ &= 2. \end{aligned}$$

The tangent line passes through $(1, 1)$ with slope $m = 2$, so its equation in point-slope form is

$$y - 1 = 2(x - 1), \quad\text{or equivalently}\quad y = 2x - 1.$$

In Section 1.3, we guessed this answer by computing secant slopes and watching them approach $2$. Now we have *proved* it.

content.title: Tangent to a cubic
content.problem: Find the slope of the tangent line to $f(x) = x^{3}$ at $x = 2$.
content.solution:
We form the difference quotient and take the limit:

$$\begin{aligned} m &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(2+h) - f(2)}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{(2+h)^{3} - 8}{h}. \end{aligned}$$

We expand the cube using the binomial expansion:

$$(2+h)^{3} = 8 + 12h + 6h^{2} + h^{3}.$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned} m &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{8 + 12h + 6h^{2} + h^{3} - 8}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{12h + 6h^{2} + h^{3}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{h(12 + 6h + h^{2})}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} (12 + 6h + h^{2}) \\ &= 12. \end{aligned}$$

The tangent line to $y = x^{3}$ at $(2, 8)$ has slope $12$.

content.text: The Derivative at a Point
The limit we have been computing—the slope of the tangent line—appears in so many contexts and with such frequency that it deserves its own name.

content.title: The Derivative of f at a
content.content: The **derivative of $f$ at $a$**, denoted $f'(a)$ (read "$f$ prime of $a$"), is $$f'(a) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h},$$ provided this limit exists. If $f'(a)$ exists, we say that $f$ is **differentiable** at $a$.

content.text: There is an equivalent formulation that is sometimes more convenient. If we write $x = a + h$, then $h = x - a$ and $h \to 0$ corresponds to $x \to a$. The definition becomes:

Both forms define the same quantity. Use whichever is more natural for the problem at hand. When the function involves $x$ directly, the alternate form often avoids heavy algebra; when the function involves a composition, the $h$-form may be simpler.

content.title: Isaac Newton (1643–1727) and Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646–1716)
content.content: The derivative was discovered independently by Isaac Newton in England and Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz in Germany during the 1660s and 1670s. Newton called derivatives *fluxions* and thought of them as instantaneous velocities of quantities "flowing" through time. Leibniz developed the $dy/dx$ notation and conceived of the derivative as a ratio of infinitesimal changes. Their approaches looked different but captured the same mathematical truth. The bitter priority dispute that followed overshadowed one of the most remarkable coincidences in intellectual history: two minds, working independently, arrived at the same revolutionary idea.

content.text: Computing Derivatives at a Point
Let us now put the definition to serious work. We compute $f'(a)$ for several fundamental functions, showing every algebraic step.

content.title: Derivative of f(x) = x² at x = a
content.problem: Find $f'(a)$ for $f(x) = x^{2}$ at an arbitrary point $x = a$.
content.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} f'(a) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{(a+h)^{2} - a^{2}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{a^{2} + 2ah + h^{2} - a^{2}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{2ah + h^{2}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{h(2a + h)}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} (2a + h) \\ &= 2a. \end{aligned}$$

For example, $f'(1) = 2$, $f'(3) = 6$, and $f'(-2) = -4$. The derivative of $x^{2}$ at any point $a$ is $2a$.

content.title: Derivative of f(x) = x³ at x = a
content.problem: Find $f'(a)$ for $f(x) = x^{3}$.
content.solution:
Using the alternate form of the definition:

$$\begin{aligned} f'(a) &= \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a} \\ &= \lim_{x \to a} \frac{x^{3} - a^{3}}{x - a}. \end{aligned}$$

We factor the numerator using the difference of cubes:

$$x^{3} - a^{3} = (x - a)(x^{2} + ax + a^{2}).$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned} f'(a) &= \lim_{x \to a} \frac{(x - a)(x^{2} + ax + a^{2})}{x - a} \\ &= \lim_{x \to a} (x^{2} + ax + a^{2}) \\ &= a^{2} + a \cdot a + a^{2} \\ &= 3a^{2}. \end{aligned}$$

For example, $f'(2) = 3(4) = 12$, which confirms our earlier calculation in Example 3.2.

content.title: Derivative of f(x) = 1/x at x = a
content.problem: Find $f'(a)$ for $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$, where $a \neq 0$.
content.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} f'(a) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\dfrac{1}{a+h} - \dfrac{1}{a}}{h}. \end{aligned}$$

We combine the fractions in the numerator using a common denominator:

$$\frac{1}{a+h} - \frac{1}{a} = \frac{a - (a+h)}{a(a+h)} = \frac{-h}{a(a+h)}.$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned} f'(a) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{h} \cdot \frac{-h}{a(a+h)} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{-1}{a(a+h)} \\ &= \frac{-1}{a \cdot a} \\ &= -\frac{1}{a^{2}}. \end{aligned}$$

The derivative of $\dfrac{1}{x}$ at $x = a$ is $-\dfrac{1}{a^{2}}$. Notice the negative sign: the function $\dfrac{1}{x}$ is *decreasing* for $x > 0$ (and also for $x < 0$), which is consistent with a negative derivative.

content.title: Derivative of f(x) = √x at x = a
content.problem: Find $f'(a)$ for $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$, where $a > 0$.
content.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} f'(a) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sqrt{a+h} - \sqrt{a}}{h}. \end{aligned}$$

Direct substitution gives $\frac{0}{0}$, so we *rationalize the numerator* by multiplying by the conjugate:

$$\begin{aligned} f'(a) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sqrt{a+h} - \sqrt{a}}{h} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{a+h} + \sqrt{a}}{\sqrt{a+h} + \sqrt{a}} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{(a+h) - a}{h\bigl(\sqrt{a+h} + \sqrt{a}\bigr)} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{h}{h\bigl(\sqrt{a+h} + \sqrt{a}\bigr)} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{\sqrt{a+h} + \sqrt{a}} \\ &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{a} + \sqrt{a}} \\ &= \frac{1}{2\sqrt{a}}. \end{aligned}$$

For example, $f'(4) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{4}} = \dfrac{1}{4}$ and $f'(9) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{9}} = \dfrac{1}{6}$. As $a$ increases, the derivative decreases—the curve $y = \sqrt{x}$ flattens out as $x$ grows, which matches its graph.

content.text: Let us collect what we have found so far:

Do you notice a pattern? The derivative of $x^{n}$ at $a$ seems to be $na^{n-1}$—this works for $n = 2$, $n = 3$, $n = -1$ (since $\frac{1}{x} = x^{-1}$ and $-1 \cdot a^{-2} = -\frac{1}{a^{2}}$), and $n = \frac{1}{2}$ (since $\frac{1}{2}a^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{a}}$). We will return to this striking pattern in Section 3.4.
Instantaneous Velocity
In Section 1.3 we studied an object moving along a straight line with position function $s(t)$. The average velocity over an interval $[a, a+h]$ was
$$v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{s(a+h) - s(a)}{h}.$$
The *instantaneous velocity* at time $t = a$ is the limit of these averages as the time interval shrinks to zero:

content.title: Instantaneous Velocity
content.content: If $s(t)$ denotes the position of an object at time $t$, then the **instantaneous velocity** at $t = a$ is $$v(a) = s'(a) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{s(a+h) - s(a)}{h},$$ provided this limit exists. The **speed** at $t = a$ is $|v(a)|$.

content.text: The derivative *is* the instantaneous velocity. This is not a metaphor or an approximation—it is an exact identity. The velocity of an object at a precise instant is the derivative of its position function at that instant.

content.title: Instantaneous velocity of a falling stone
content.problem: A stone is dropped from a 400-foot cliff. Its height after $t$ seconds is $s(t) = 400 - 16t^{2}$. Find the instantaneous velocity at $t = 3$ seconds.
content.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} v(3) = s'(3) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{s(3+h) - s(3)}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{[400 - 16(3+h)^{2}] - [400 - 16(9)]}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{400 - 16(9 + 6h + h^{2}) - 400 + 144}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{-144 - 96h - 16h^{2} + 144}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{-96h - 16h^{2}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} (-96 - 16h) \\ &= -96. \end{aligned}$$

The instantaneous velocity at $t = 3$ is $-96$ ft/s. The negative sign indicates that the stone is moving *downward* (its height is decreasing). Its speed at that instant is $|-96| = 96$ ft/s.

content.text: Rates of Change in Other Contexts
The tangent problem and the velocity problem are two faces of the same coin: both ask for an instantaneous rate of change. The derivative provides a unified answer.

content.title: Instantaneous Rate of Change
content.content: The **instantaneous rate of change** of $f$ at $a$ is $f'(a)$. Equivalently, $f'(a)$ is the slope of the tangent line to $y = f(x)$ at $x = a$.

content.text: The derivative can represent velocity, growth rate, marginal cost, reaction rate, or any quantity that measures how fast an output changes with respect to an input. The interpretation depends on the context; the mathematics is always the same.

content.title: Rate of change of area
content.problem: A circular oil spill on still water has radius $r$ meters. Its area is $A(r) = \pi r^{2}$. Find the rate at which the area changes with respect to the radius when $r = 5$ meters.
content.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} A'(5) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{A(5+h) - A(5)}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\pi(5+h)^{2} - 25\pi}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\pi(25 + 10h + h^{2}) - 25\pi}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{10\pi h + \pi h^{2}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} (10\pi + \pi h) \\ &= 10\pi. \end{aligned}$$

When the radius is 5 meters, the area is increasing at a rate of $10\pi \approx 31.4$ square meters per meter of radius. Each additional meter of radius adds roughly $31.4\,\text{m}^{2}$ to the spill.

content.text: Tangent Lines and Normal Lines
We have defined the tangent line as the line through $(a, f(a))$ with slope $f'(a)$. The **normal line** at the same point is perpendicular to the tangent line.

content.title: Normal Line
content.content: The **normal line** to $y = f(x)$ at $(a, f(a))$ is the line through $(a, f(a))$ perpendicular to the tangent line. If $f'(a) \neq 0$, the normal line has slope $-\dfrac{1}{f'(a)}$. If $f'(a) = 0$, the tangent line is horizontal and the normal line is the vertical line $x = a$.

content.title: Tangent and normal lines to 1/x
content.problem: Find the equations of the tangent line and the normal line to $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ at the point $(2, \tfrac{1}{2})$.
content.solution:
From Example 3.5, we know that $f'(a) = -\dfrac{1}{a^{2}}$, so

$$f'(2) = -\frac{1}{4}.$$

The tangent line through $(2, \frac{1}{2})$ with slope $-\frac{1}{4}$ is

$$y - \tfrac{1}{2} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(x - 2), \quad\text{or equivalently}\quad y = -\tfrac{1}{4}x + 1.$$

The normal line has slope $-\dfrac{1}{f'(2)} = -\dfrac{1}{-1/4} = 4$, so

$$y - \tfrac{1}{2} = 4(x - 2), \quad\text{or equivalently}\quad y = 4x - \tfrac{15}{2}.$$

content.title: Tangent line to the square root function
content.problem: Find the equation of the tangent line to $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ at $(4, 2)$.
content.solution:
From Example 3.6, $f'(a) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{a}}$, so

$$f'(4) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{4}} = \frac{1}{4}.$$

The tangent line is

$$y - 2 = \tfrac{1}{4}(x - 4), \quad\text{or}\quad y = \tfrac{1}{4}x + 1.$$

content.text: When Does the Derivative Fail to Exist?
The derivative $f'(a)$ is defined as a limit, and limits do not always exist. There are four common geometric situations in which a function fails to be differentiable at a point.
**1. Corner.** The function $f(x) = |x|$ has a sharp corner at $x = 0$. The left-hand secant slopes approach $-1$ while the right-hand secant slopes approach $+1$:
Since the one-sided limits disagree, $f'(0)$ does not exist.
**2. Cusp.** The function $f(x) = x^{2/3}$ has a cusp at $x = 0$. The difference quotient

tends to $+\infty$ as $h \to 0^{+}$ and to $-\infty$ as $h \to 0^{-}$. The limit does not exist (even as an infinite limit), so $f'(0)$ is undefined. Geometrically, the tangent line would be vertical, but the curve approaches it from different sides.
**3. Vertical tangent.** The function $f(x) = x^{1/3}$ has a vertical tangent at $x = 0$:
$$\frac{f(0+h) - f(0)}{h} = \frac{h^{1/3}}{h} = \frac{1}{h^{2/3}} \to +\infty \quad\text{as } h \to 0.$$
The one-sided limits both tend to $+\infty$, so the tangent line is vertical: $x = 0$. Since the slope is not a real number, $f'(0)$ does not exist.

**4. Discontinuity.** If $f$ is not continuous at $a$, then $f$ is certainly not differentiable at $a$. (We will prove this in Section 3.2.) For example, the Heaviside step function $H(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & x < 0 \\ 1 & x \ge 0 \end{cases}$ has a jump discontinuity at $x = 0$ and is not differentiable there.

content.title: Differentiable is "stronger" than continuous
content.content: Every differentiable function is continuous (Theorem 3.2 in Section 3.2), but a continuous function need not be differentiable. The function $f(x) = |x|$ is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at $x = 0$.

content.title: Where is f(x) = |x − 3| not differentiable?
content.problem: Determine where $f(x) = |x - 3|$ fails to be differentiable.
content.solution:
We can write

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} -(x-3) = 3 - x & \text{if } x < 3, \\ x - 3 & \text{if } x \ge 3. \end{cases}$$

For $x \neq 3$, the function is a linear function on each piece, so it is differentiable there. At $x = 3$:

$$\lim_{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{f(3+h) - f(3)}{h} = \lim_{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{|h|}{h} = \lim_{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{-h}{h} = -1,$$

$$\lim_{h \to 0^{+}} \frac{f(3+h) - f(3)}{h} = \lim_{h \to 0^{+}} \frac{h}{h} = 1.$$

Since $-1 \neq 1$, the limit does not exist and $f'(3)$ does not exist. The function has a corner at $x = 3$.

content.title: Finding the Derivative at a Point
content.content:
To compute $f'(a)$:

**1.** Write the difference quotient $\dfrac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h}$.

**2.** Simplify the expression algebraically (expand, factor, rationalize, combine fractions) until you can cancel the $h$ in the denominator.

**3.** Evaluate $\lim_{h \to 0}$ of the simplified expression.

*Alternatively*, use the form $\dfrac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a}$ and take $\lim_{x \to a}$. Choose whichever form leads to simpler algebra.

content.title: A derivative that does not exist
content.problem: Show that $f(x) = (x - 1)^{2/3}$ is not differentiable at $x = 1$.
content.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} \frac{f(1+h) - f(1)}{h} &= \frac{(1+h-1)^{2/3} - 0}{h} \\ &= \frac{h^{2/3}}{h} \\ &= \frac{1}{h^{1/3}}. \end{aligned}$$

As $h \to 0^{+}$, $h^{1/3} \to 0^{+}$, so $1/h^{1/3} \to +\infty$. As $h \to 0^{-}$, $h^{1/3} \to 0^{-}$, so $1/h^{1/3} \to -\infty$. The limit does not exist, and $f'(1)$ is undefined. The graph has a cusp at $(1, 0)$.

content.text: Exercises
Derivative at a Point

content.problem: Use the limit definition to find $f'(2)$ for $f(x) = 3x + 5$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(3)$ for $f(x) = x^{2}$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(-1)$ for $f(x) = x^{2} + 2x$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(1)$ for $f(x) = x^{3}$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(2)$ for $f(x) = x^{3}$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(4)$ for $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(9)$ for $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(3)$ for $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(-2)$ for $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(1)$ for $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x^{2}}$. *Hint:* Combine fractions in the numerator carefully.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(2)$ for $f(x) = x^{2} - 3x + 1$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(a)$ for $f(x) = 5x^{2} - x$.

content.text: Tangent and Normal Lines

content.problem: Find the equation of the tangent line to $f(x) = x^{2}$ at the point $(3, 9)$.
Find the equation of the tangent line to $f(x) = x^{3}$ at the point $(-1, -1)$.
Find the equations of both the tangent and normal lines to $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ at $(9, 3)$.
Find the equation of the tangent line to $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ at $(1, 1)$.
Find the equation of the normal line to $f(x) = x^{2}$ at the point $(2, 4)$.
At what point on the curve $y = x^{2}$ is the tangent line parallel to the line $y = 6x - 1$?
At what point on the curve $y = x^{2}$ is the tangent line perpendicular to the line $y = \dfrac{1}{4}x + 3$?
Find all points on the curve $y = x^{3}$ where the tangent line has slope $12$.

content.text: Velocity Problems

content.problem: A ball is thrown upward from the ground with initial velocity $48$ ft/s. Its height is $s(t) = 48t - 16t^{2}$ feet after $t$ seconds. Find the instantaneous velocity at $t = 1$.
Using the position function from the previous problem, find the velocity at $t = \dfrac{3}{2}$ (the peak). What does the answer tell you?
A car's position along a straight road is $s(t) = t^{3} - 6t^{2} + 9t$ meters, $t \ge 0$ seconds. Find $s'(1)$ and $s'(3)$. Interpret each.
A rocket's height is $h(t) = 200t - 4.9t^{2}$ meters. Find the velocity at $t = 10$ seconds using the limit definition.
An object moves along a line with position $s(t) = \dfrac{1}{t+1}$ meters at time $t \ge 0$ seconds. Find $s'(2)$ using the limit definition.

content.text: Rates of Change

content.problem: The area of a square with side length $s$ is $A(s) = s^{2}$. Find the rate of change of area with respect to side length when $s = 4$.
The volume of a cube with edge length $s$ is $V(s) = s^{3}$. Find $V'(3)$ using the limit definition and interpret the result.

content.text: Challenging Problems

content.problem: Use the limit definition to find $f'(a)$ for $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x^{2}}$, where $a \neq 0$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(a)$ for $f(x) = \sqrt{2x + 1}$, where $a > -\tfrac{1}{2}$. *Hint:* Rationalize the numerator.
Find the equation of the tangent line to $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ at the point $(a, 1/a)$, and determine the value of $a$ for which this tangent line passes through the origin.
Show that the tangent line to $y = x^{2}$ at the point $(a, a^{2})$ intersects the $x$-axis at $x = \dfrac{a}{2}$.
The tangent line to $y = \sqrt{x}$ at the point $(a, \sqrt{a})$, $a > 0$, has $x$-intercept and $y$-intercept. Find both in terms of $a$.

content.text: Proofs

content.problem:
Show that the two forms of the derivative definition are equivalent:

$$\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h} = \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a}$$

by making the substitution $x = a + h$.
Let $f(x) = cx + d$ be a linear function. Prove from the limit definition that $f'(a) = c$ for every $a$.

Suppose $f'(a)$ exists and $f'(a) > 0$. Prove that there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $f(x) > f(a)$ for all $x$ in $(a, a+\delta)$. *Hint:* Use the definition of the limit and choose $\varepsilon = f'(a)$.

content.text: Creation Reveals

content.problem: A population of bacteria in a petri dish is modeled by $P(t) = 500 + 40t^{2}$ cells after $t$ hours. Use the limit definition to find the instantaneous growth rate $P'(3)$. Express your answer in cells per hour and interpret.

The depth of water (in meters) in a tidal pool $t$ hours after midnight is modeled by $D(t) = 4 - \dfrac{1}{t+1}$ for $t \ge 0$. Find $D'(3)$ using the limit definition. Is the water depth increasing or decreasing at $t = 3$?
